Spring-Senglaup, Karen Diane-Enc #9001891424-OPT-SSC-4/9/2026 FACE SHEET REGISTRATION FORM 3/26/2026 1 pg
INS VERIFIED SUBSCRIBER RLTN NOC REF NUMBER
HPI: This is a 70 year old female who:
1. is being seen for evaluation of right hip pain. Onset or Duration: 10 years. Symptoms
began with a fall. Symptoms include groin pain, hip instability, and limited mobility. The
patient describes symptoms as constant and radiating. At their worst, symptoms are rated
as 10 out of 10. Symptoms worsen with repetitive activity. She has had no diagnostic
studies. Previous treatment has included cane.
Right Hip: skin intact, no rashes or lesions..
Right Hip: Unable to get accurate leg
Russell T Nevins, MD (Primary Provider) (Bill Under)
The following images were ordered and obtained:
Additional Images: RT HIP 2V WB AP & LAT
RIGHT, Initial: Hip, Unilateral; 2-3 views; CPT 73502
Specific Findings: Loss of femoral head, cysts in the femoral head and acetabulum
Bone on bone joint space narrowing, increased sclerosis, Osteophyte formation
Musculoskeletal care: I advised the patient that most patients with hip DJD can achieve some measure of pain relief
from nonsurgical options like lifestyle modification, exercise and physical therapy, and use of supportive devices. The
importance of maintaining an ideal body weight to reduce stress on the hip joint was emphasized.
Expectations: I counseled the patient about the natural history of DJD which typically has exacerbations and
remissions. Many people report that changes in the weather also affect the degree of pain from arthritis. For some,
conservative management is not satisfactory. These individuals should consider surgical options.
Contact Office if your hip arthritis pain worsens, or if the hip becomes warm or accompanied by a fever.
NSAIDS : I discussed with the patient that NSAIDs should be taken with food. Prolonged use of NSAIDs can result in
the development of stomach ulcers or bleeding. Patient advised to stop taking NSAIDs if abdominal pain occurs. The
patient verbalized understanding of the proper use and possible adverse effects of NSAIDs. All of the patient's
NSAIDs: Meloxicam to be taken with food.
Active Therapy : Active therapies include stretching exercises, weight training (quadriceps strengthening), and
Passive Therapy : Passive therapies include ice, heat, massage, ultrasound, and electrical stimulation. Your therapist
will decide which of these interventions will be most beneficial to you.
Intraarticular steroid injection : I discussed with the patient that this involves the injection of a potent antiinflammatory
substance (cortisone) into the hip joint. Risks include whitening of the skin at the injection site and a transient rise in
blood glucose. Complications are extremely rare; infection is the most common. There is no rule as to how many
Russell T Nevins, MD (Primary Provider) (Bill Under)
injections can be given. Because some research has shown that too much cortisone can damage cartilage, most
physicians limit the number of shots they will give you.
Total hip replacement : I explained to the patient that this is an option for patients that have severe pain in their hip
from arthritis who have failed conservative management. The diseased hip socket and femoral head (the ball) are
replaced with a combination of either metal, plastic, ceramic, or a combination of these. The vast majority of patients
who undergo this surgery have a significant reduction in pain and a dramatic improvement in their ability to do
common activities of daily living. High impact activities should be avoided after surgery. Your surgeon might also have
you avoid specific positions that might put you at risk for a dislocation. Risks include but are not limited to infection,
injury to blood vessels and nerves, blood clots, fracture, dislocation, leg length discrepancy, and cardiopulmonary
I reviewed and discussed the results of the X-Ray(s) with the patient.
After discussing the risks, benefits and alternatives, we decided on the following plan for the RIGHT: TOTAL HIP
REPLACEMENT and THE PATIENT WAS GIVEN. HANDOUT ABOUT TOTAL HIP REPLACEMENT WITH ALL THE
Risks, benefits and alternatives were discussed with the patient including the risks of blood clot, infection, stroke,
heart attack, death, disability, loss of range of motion, stiffness, skin healing problems, nerve damage, blood vessel
damage, bleeding, leg length discrepancy, fractures, tendon damage, dislocation, persistent pain, swelling,
amputation, continued pain requiring further surgery. No guarantees were given and/or implied for complete pain
relief. Patient understand all the risks, benefits, and alternatives to surgical intervention.
Follow up for: Pre-op visit. Other Instructions: Schedule pre-op visit with Viet Nguyen PA-C
after all medical clearance has been completed for Right total hip replacement
The patient comes in today for evaluation of right hip pain. She states 10 years ago she had increasing right groin pain for
about 6 months which later resided. The pain has returned over the past 2 years and has to use a cane for ambulation.
She describes the pain as being in the right groin, thigh and radiating down the leg. She states Dr Winder is treating her
The patient has severe end stage Right hip osteoarthritis
On physical examination, the patient is almost auto fused.
Treatment options discussed are to continue with behavior modification, intra articular hip injection every 6 months,
physical therapy, Non steroidal anti-inflammatories, and Total Hip replacement
I also had a lengthy conversation with the patient and her sister that she is at higher risk of falls due to her fused hips and
osteoarthritis of bilateral knee. I have recommended she consider a Life Alert.
I have also discussed the patient is at higher risk of a leg length discrepancy following total hip replacement due to auto
fused right hip with contractures making it difficult to measure leg lengths
The patient has failed conservative treatement
Russell T Nevins, MD (Primary Provider) (Bill Under)
The plan is to proceed with a right total hip replacement
Russell T Nevins, MD (Primary Provider) (Bill Under)
Lisa Marciniak scribing for, and in the presence of, Russell Nevins, MD.
I, Russell Nevins, MD, personally performed the services described in the documentation as scribed by Lisa Marciniak in
my presence, and I confirm the documentation is both accurate and complete.

Name of Patient (as it appears on driver's license)
If prior covid history, was infection:
If symptomatic: Was patient hospitalized for covid?
If symptomatic: Is/was patient diabetic or immunocompromised?
If hospitalized, was patient in ICU?
Name of Insured (as it appears on driver's license)
Diagnosis and ICD-10 Code M16.11 RIGHT HIP OSTEOARTHRITIS
Procedure as it is to appear on the Consent (include surgical site and side / no abbreviations) & CPT Code
Pre-Admission Testing Orders If pretests obtained/completed elsewhere, indicate:
CMP On admission, if patient has a history of renal or liver disease
Blood Glucose Level (POC) on admission, if patient has a history of diabetes
Urine Pregnancy Test on all females of child-bearing age (who have had a period in the last year)
Tranexamic Acid 1 gram I.V. prior to incision & 1 gram I.V. before closing incision. (Do not give if history of Glaucoma)
Hx of renal failure: NS 500mL at 20mL/hr
Pre-Operative Antimicrobial agent: Administer x1 dose within 1 hour prior to surgery (2 hours for vancomycin or fluoroquinolones)
Biliary, Cardiac, Gastroduodenal, Hernia, Neurosurgery, Orthopedic, Plastic Surgery, Thoracic, Vascular:
Appendectomy, Colorectal, Head/Neck, Hysterectomy, S. Intestine:
**CEFAZOLIN 2G IV or 3G FOR PATIENT WEIGHT > 120 KG
-Patient known to be colonized with MRSA, it is reasonable to add a single PREOP dose of vancomycin MAX DOSE 2G -
This is a 70 year old female who:
is being seen for evaluation of right hip pain. Onset or Duration: 10 years. Symptoms
began with a fall. Symptoms include groin pain, hip instability, and limited mobility. The
patient describes symptoms as constant and radiating. At their worst, symptoms are
as 10 out of 10. Symptoms worsen with repetitive activity. She has had no diagnostic
studies. Previous treatment has included cane.
tablet) 10 Milligram 1 Tabs By Mouth Daily
tablet) 20 Milligram 1 Tabs By Mouth Daily
Right Hip: skin intact, no rashes or lesions..
Right Hip: Unable to get accurate leg
right hip osteoarthritis - plan is for right total hip arthroplasty.
Risks, benefits and alternatives were discussed with the patient including the risks of
blood clot, infection, stroke, heart attack, death, disability, loss of range of motion,
stiffness, skin healing problems, nerve damage, blood vessel damage, tendon damage,
persistent pain, swelling, limp, fractures, leg length discrepancy, continued pain
requiring further surgery up to and including revision. No guarantees were given and/or
implied for complete pain relief. Patient agrees to proceed with surgery.
Attending Dr: Nevins MD, Russell T
D/C to Home W Home Health
Home w/ home health. DME order: front wheel walker, 3-1 commode. Total hip arthroplasty. need
until 99 Nurse: ASA 81mg BID X 6 weeks. mepilex intact x7 d, then remove shower with zipties.
Home PT, Strengthening and dislocation precautions. Rehab consult.
Home health with skilled RN for incision care, medication management, and assessment. Home PT
eval/treat gait, ROM, strengthening. Home oT eval/treat ADL training.
